title: Conversion of long-term monoculture plantation to agroforestry is beneficial for increasing soil carbon storage in karst yellow soil areas.
author_string: Shi, Bingyang, Wang, Xiurong, Pan, Yuanhai, Liu, Fuhua, Fu, Tianyu et al.
abstract: The transition from monoculture plantations to agroforestry systems can significantly alter soil fertility and the composition of soil microbial communities, thereby influencing soil carbon cycling processes. However, the specific effects of soil microbial communities and carbon cycling functional genes on carbon storage within agroforestry systems, particularly in karst regions, remain incompletely understood. This study evaluated the influence of three prevalent Gleditsia sinensis-crop agroforestry systems in karst yellow soil areas on soil carbon storage, microbial communities, and the abundance of carbon cycling functional genes. The results indicated that long-term agroforestry systems significantly increased surface soil (0-20 cm depth) organic carbon (SOC) content and urease (SUE) activity (p < 0.01). Notably, these agroforestry systems reduced the diversity of microbial species associated with the carbon cycle and significantly altered their microbiome composition. Furthermore, the agroforestry systems significantly decreased the abundance of genes associated with methane metabolism and the starch and lignin degradation pathways. Among them, SUE, microbial biomass carbon (MBC), acid protease (SACPT) activity, SOC and pH were the primary determinants of carbon cycling microorganisms and functional gene abundance (p < 0.01). The abundance of functional genes related to methane metabolism, starch, and lignin metabolism emerged as the most influential factor governing soil carbon storage. In conclusion, the G. sinensis-crop agroforestry systems, by decreasing the abundance of genes involved in methane metabolism, starch, and lignin degradation, decelerating carbon decomposition rates, thereby enhancing carbon storage. These findings provide critical insights for managing carbon pools in agroforestry systems within karst regions and provide essential theoretical support for the development of sustainable forest management models.
